content:
Where Do People Get This From?

A few nights ago, I wrote a blog post.

The idea was simple: web development isn't dying, it's growing. I posted it on Dev.to, Medium, and LinkedIn, feeling pretty good about it.

Then, that same night, I came across another blog post.

Same topic. Same core idea.

But the way it was written, the way it painted the picture, the way it explained things... it was far better than mine.

I sat there for a moment just staring at the screen thinking, "Where do people get this from?"

This idea. This way of seeing things. This way of putting sentences together so they actually land.

I honestly don't remember the person's name anymore. I think it was on LinkedIn. But I remember exactly how I felt reading it.

Amazed. And a little embarrassed, if I'm honest.

Not because my post was bad. But because theirs made mine look like a rough draft.

My first instinct was to compare. To wonder if I even had anything worth saying if someone else could say it that much better.

But then I sat with it a bit longer, and a different thought showed up.

That person didn't wake up writing like that.

Somewhere behind that one blog post is a pile of other posts nobody ever saw. Drafts that got deleted. Ideas that didn't land. Years of reading, writing, rewriting, and probably getting told their early stuff wasn't great either.

What I saw was the output. I didn't see the process.

And that's the part that's easy to forget when you're the one still early in yours.

Time does something writing tips can't teach you. So does consistency. You can read every "how to write better" article on the internet, but at some point, the only thing that actually improves your writing is writing. A lot of it. Over and over. Badly, at first.

The same thing is true for code. For design. For basically any craft I can think of.

The developer whose code looks effortless wrote a lot of broken code first. The designer whose layouts feel obvious spent years making cluttered ones. The person whose blog post painted a picture so clearly probably has a folder full of posts that didn't.

So here's what I took from that night.

Instead of asking "why can't I write like that yet," I started asking "what would it take for me to write like that eventually."

And the honest answer is: time, reps, and not quitting when my own stuff feels average compared to someone else's.

That's really it. There's no shortcut hiding somewhere that they found and I haven't.

So if you're building something, whether it's writing, code, design, or anything else, and you come across someone doing it better than you right now, let it inspire you instead of discourage you.

They were where you are once too.

You're just seeing them a few thousand hours ahead of you.

Keep writing. Keep building. Keep showing up.

The gap closes slower than you want, but it does close.
